result: The art of losing isn’t hard to master, as Elizabeth Bishop wrote, but I’ve always found the smaller disappearances harder to accept than the grand ones. A lost sock is a tiny, maddening betrayal of order. A missing pen, the one that fit your hand perfectly, leaves a vague mourning. These objects vanish into a domestic void, a parallel dimension that hoards keys and single earrings, feeding on our inattention. We misplace days, too—hours dissolved in the scroll, minutes lost to anxious loops of thought. That’s the truer vanishing act: not the things themselves, but the selves we mislay along the way. The version of you that painted in the afternoon, or the one who listened without thinking of what to say next. They slip out quietly, and one day you realize the house of your mind has rooms you no longer enter. But perhaps loss is not just absence. Perhaps it’s a kind of harvest, a winnowing. The lost things carve a shape of what remains, giving contour to what we still hold. I think of the Japanese art of kintsugi, repairing broken pottery with gold—the scar becomes the story. So maybe we are not diminished but focused. The missing sock becomes a rag for dusting; the lost pen, a faint memory that nudges you to find a new favorite. And the hours we dissipated? They teach us the weight of the ones we keep. Losing, then, is a forgotten skill, a way of learning to treasure without clutching, to hold what’s here as if it were already memory—precious because temporary, beautiful because it can be misplaced.
